stories.headline: Costilla County Sheriff, Four Deputies Indicted on Charges of Corpse Abuse and Assault
stories.summary:
Two grand juries in Colorado's 12th Judicial District indicted five members of the Costilla County Sheriff's Office on Thursday in separate cases involving the improper handling of human remains and the assault of a citizen. Sheriff Danny Sanchez and former deputy Keith Schultz face charges of abuse of a corpse and second-degree official misconduct after allegedly collecting partial human remains in a paper bag, leaving additional remains at the scene, and failing to notify the coroner.

The indictment alleges Sanchez and Schultz responded to a report of human remains at Mild Horse Mesa on Oct. 2, 2024, but Sanchez was described as more interested in looking for arrowheads than conducting a proper investigation. Schultz later wrote a police report for the incident — dated Dec. 31, 2024 — that failed to document a large number of remains. A coroner from a neighboring county testified that the deputies' actions were disrespectful to the decedent and violated statutory requirements.

In a separate indictment, Undersheriff Cruz Soto, Sgt. Caleb Sanchez (the sheriff's son), and Deputy Roland Riley face charges for allegedly breaking the rib of a Costilla County resident who had been cleared following a mental health crisis on Feb. 3. The resident was pursued by deputies after leaving the scene. The alleged mishandling of the remains came to light after the original reporting party found additional bones at the site in August 2025 and contacted the district attorney's office directly.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC Closes Land Acquisition at Santa Fe Yards for Future Stadium
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC announced Thursday that it has successfully closed its land acquisition at Santa Fe Yards, a significant milestone for the National Women's Soccer League club ahead of its planned permanent home. The city of Denver committed $50 million to purchase the stadium land and $20 million for area improvements as part of the deal.

The announcement comes one day before the team's first-ever home match, scheduled Saturday at Empower Field at Mile High, where the club expects a crowd exceeding 60,000 fans. Head Coach Nick Cushing said he wants players to embrace the moment, telling them the opportunity to perform before such a crowd is what young athletes dream of.

Controlling Owner Rob Cohen said the community's response has exceeded the organization's expectations since the club launched 15 months ago. Forward Janine Sonis, a Colorado native, called the prospect of playing before a near-capacity crowd at Empower Field 'pretty unbelievable.'

stories.headline: Avalanche Beat Jets 3-2 on MacKinnon's Pair of Third-Period Goals, Extend Win Streak to Four
stories.summary:
Nathan MacKinnon scored twice in the third period to lift the Colorado Avalanche to a 3-2 victory over the Winnipeg Jets on Thursday, extending Colorado's winning streak to four games. The Avalanche entered the final frame tied 1-1 before MacKinnon struck twice in the opening four minutes of the period to put the team ahead 3-1.

Mackenzie Blackwood made 22 saves to secure the win. The fourth line of Parker Kelly, Jack Drury, and Joel Kiviranta drew praise for a persistent forecheck that created sustained pressure on Jets defenders. Drury's first-period goal — poked through while sprawled on the ice — exemplified what the team described as a night of 'ugly but effective' offense.

The victory pushes Colorado nine points ahead of the Dallas Stars in the Central Division standings, making the Avalanche a virtual lock to claim the division title. The team is also returning to full health, with recent comebacks from Gabriel Landeskog, Logan O'Connor, Ross Colton, and Artturi Lehkonen, who assisted on MacKinnon's second goal in his first game back from an upper-body injury.

stories.headline: Rockies Lose Season Opener 2-1 to Marlins, Fail to Capitalize on Scoring Opportunities
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies dropped their 2026 season opener Friday night in Miami, falling 2-1 to the Marlins at loanDepot Park. The loss echoed the struggles that plagued the franchise through three consecutive 100-loss seasons, as Colorado repeatedly failed to deliver key hits with runners in scoring position.

Veteran left-hander Kyle Freeland started his fifth Opening Day and held Miami to two runs over 4 1/3 innings, keeping the Rockies competitive. Sandy Alcantara pitched seven efficient innings for Miami, needing only 73 pitches to limit Colorado's offense. New second baseman Willi Castro went 0-for-4 with two strikeouts in his Rockies debut.

Colorado's lone run came in the fourth inning on an infield single by Jordan Beck, but the Rockies stranded the bases loaded when Alcantara struck out the next batter. Colorado also saw Jake McCarthy thrown out at the plate trying to score from second, and Beck was caught stealing in the seventh. Manager Warren Schaeffer had pledged an aggressive baserunning approach on the road this season.

stories.headline: Smoke from Nebraska Wildfires Blankets Denver Metro, Sparking Flood of 911 Calls
stories.summary:
A thick haze descended on the Denver metro area Thursday afternoon, originating from the Ashby and Minor wildfires burning in the Sandhills of Nebraska, according to the National Weather Service in Boulder. A strong cold front pushing from the northeast drove the smoke plume into Colorado, causing visibility restrictions near Fort Morgan and Akron before spreading to the metro.

Emergency dispatch centers were overwhelmed with concerned callers. South Metro Fire Rescue and officials in Broomfield, Elbert County, and other jurisdictions issued public notices urging residents not to call 911 unless they saw active flames. Adams County fire crews traveled to Nebraska to assist in fighting one of the fires Thursday evening.

Forecasters said the smoke was expected to clear as the cold front pushed through the region, potentially bringing light precipitation to the Denver metro overnight. The episode added another layer of air quality concern to a region already on edge due to record-low snowpack and elevated fire danger across the Front Range.

stories.headline: Denver Water Stage 1 Restrictions Now in Effect; Guide to Metro-Wide Drought Rules
stories.summary:
Denver Water's Stage 1 drought restrictions, unanimously approved by the utility's board and effective March 25, are now in force for the 1.5 million residents it serves across Denver and surrounding suburbs. It marks the first time Stage 1 restrictions have been imposed since 2013, driven by snowpack levels that the utility described as at or near record lows as of March 23.

Under the restrictions, lawn watering is limited to two designated days per week based on address number, and no grass watering is allowed between 10 a.m. and 6 p.m. Restaurants may only serve water upon request, and hotels must limit linen washing unless guests request it. Trees, shrubs, perennials, and vegetables may be hand-watered any day outside those hours.

Other Front Range communities have enacted or are preparing their own restrictions. Arvada has declared a drought and is aligning with Denver Water rules pending formal enactment of its own ordinance. Thornton and Erie are also under restrictions. Denver Water said both the Colorado River and South Platte River watershed snowpack rankings are at their worst on record, calling conditions 'highly concerning.'

stories.headline: Jefferson County Adopts Updated Wildfire Resiliency Code Ahead of State Deadline
stories.summary:
Jefferson County commissioners approved updates to the county's Wildfire Resiliency Code on March 10, with the changes set to take effect July 1. The updates bring Jefferson County into compliance with a state-mandated code established July 1, 2025, which carried an April 1 deadline for local jurisdictions. Jefferson County is ranked as having higher wildfire risk than 98% of counties in the United States, according to Wildfirerisk.org.

Key changes include requiring new structures in the Wildland Urban Interface Overlay District to obtain permits from certified fire specialists verifying defensible space — cleared vegetation around buildings — and confirming that road and driveway access meets emergency vehicle standards. Those permits expire every three years, and property owners are legally required to maintain vegetation clearance. New home construction must also use fire-resistant materials, noncombustible gutters, properly ventilated attics, and ignition-resistant decking.

Commissioner Lesley Dahlkemper cited record-low snowpack and unseasonably high temperatures as factors heightening urgency. West Metro Fire spokesperson Ronda Scholting called the adoption 'a vital step.' Officials noted that even with the new codes in place, homeowners remain responsible for maintaining their own properties, planning evacuations, and registering for county emergency notification systems.

stories.headline: Front Range Passenger Rail Backers Weigh November Ballot Measure to Expand Service to Pueblo
stories.summary:
Officials and advocates for the Front Range Passenger Rail held a public town hall in downtown Denver on Wednesday as they gauge community support for a potential November ballot measure that would raise sales taxes to expand the planned commuter rail system. The starter service — funded by RTD and state transportation fees — is expected to launch by 2029 with three daily round trips between Denver and Fort Collins, and will finally deliver a long-promised Denver-to-Boulder connection from the FasTracks program.

The proposed ballot measure would extend service south to Colorado Springs and Pueblo and increase the number of daily trips to ten by 2045. Front Range Passenger Rail District Board Chair John Putnam framed the initiative partly as a trust-rebuilding exercise after FasTracks, a multi-billion-dollar transit expansion approved by voters in 2004, remains incomplete more than two decades later.

Public skepticism was evident at the town hall, with attendees raising questions about RTD's ongoing financial troubles — including a $215 million budget deficit that is prompting service cuts — and unresolved specifics around funding and implementation. Officials said more details on the financial plan would be finalized in the coming months before any ballot measure is formally pursued. The current framework calls for a targeted sales tax increase in communities along the rail corridor rather than across the entire district.

stories.headline: Colorado Officials Move to Repeal and Replace the Colorado AI Act Before Its June Deadline
stories.summary:
Governor Jared Polis released a draft bill on March 17 that would substantially overhaul the Colorado AI Act, replacing its core requirements with a narrower framework focused on transparency, disclosure, and consumer notice for automated decision-making technology. The proposal has not yet been formally introduced in the General Assembly but marks the latest effort to reform the law ahead of its June 30, 2026, effective date.

As enacted in May 2024, the Colorado AI Act requires developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems to exercise a duty of reasonable care to prevent algorithmic discrimination and to conduct impact assessments and risk management reviews. The draft bill would scrap those provisions entirely, favoring lighter-touch disclosure obligations more closely aligned with California's consumer privacy regulations.

The proposal narrows the definition of covered entities and adds exemptions not present in the original law, including for developers building AI solely for internal use. It also expands the definition of 'consumer' to include employees and job applicants. Critics and supporters alike are awaiting further details as the bill remains in draft form, with the clock ticking on an effective date that affects AI companies operating across Colorado.

stories.headline: Denver Offers Grants of Up to $3,600 to Help Restaurants Transition to Composting Ahead of September Mandate
stories.summary:
The city of Denver is making $51,000 available in grants to help local restaurants cover the cost of compost hauling services for one year, ahead of a Sept. 1 deadline that will require all city restaurants to contract with a licensed food-waste hauler. The city's Office of Climate Action, Sustainability and Resiliency selected ten restaurants to receive up to $3,600 each for composting costs, and fifteen additional restaurants are eligible for up to $1,000 for reusable tableware.

Applicants must request funding for reusable items — glasses, utensils, plates — as part of the same application to qualify for composting assistance. Colorado-based consulting firm Diversion Designers will serve as a liaison for approved restaurants, auditing current waste practices, gathering hauler quotes, and guiding operators through the transition. Compost hauling services typically run between $200 and $300 per month, according to local provider Scraps Mile High.

The September requirement stems from a 2022 ballot measure and applies to food and drink establishments with dine-in service within city limits. The Colorado Restaurant Association said it was not surprised Denver was offering help to ease the transition, noting that similar incentives have been offered in other Front Range cities including Boulder. The application portal will remain open until funds are exhausted.

stories.headline: Frozen Dead Guy Days Returns to Estes Park This Weekend With New Events and Drone Show Finale
stories.summary:
Frozen Dead Guy Days, one of Colorado's most unusual annual festivals, returns to Estes Park from Friday, March 27, through Sunday, March 29, drawing visitors with coffin races, costumed revelry, and live music. The festival honors 'Grandpa' Bredo Morstoel, a Norwegian man who died in 1989 and whose cryonically preserved body has resided in an icehouse at the Stanley Hotel since August 2023.

This year's event introduces several new attractions, including a Cannibal Chase 8K run and a commemorative drone show finale produced in partnership with the Colorado Tourism Office's America 250 – Colorado 150 initiative, celebrating the nation's 250th anniversary and Colorado's 150th year of statehood. The Aspen & Evergreen gallery's Dead of Winter Art Exhibit, inspired by the festival, will also be displayed at the event on March 28.

Returning highlights include the signature coffin races, with 30 teams navigating an obstacle course in elaborately designed coffins, as well as the Royal Blue Ball and Polar Plunge Pavilion Party at the Stanley Hotel and a festival bar crawl. The event is expected to draw large crowds to the mountain town on the eastern edge of Rocky Mountain National Park.

weather.notable: About 27 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I think they named oranges before they named carrots. 'Hey, what are those?' 'Those are orange... oranges.' 'What about those?' 'Ah, those are... orange... long pointies.'

topStory.headline: Iran-Linked Hackers Breach FBI Director Kash Patel's Personal Email, Publish Photos and Documents
topStory.summary:
Iran-linked hackers broke into the personal Gmail account of FBI Director Kash Patel and published more than 300 emails, personal photographs and other documents on the internet, the hacker group and the FBI confirmed Friday. The group, known as Handala Hack Team, posted images of Patel smoking cigars, riding in an antique convertible and taking a selfie with a large bottle of rum, along with a cache of personal and work correspondence dating from 2010 to 2019.

The FBI confirmed the breach in a statement from spokesperson Ben Williamson, who said the bureau had "taken all necessary steps to mitigate potential risks associated with this activity" and characterized the exposed data as "historical in nature" with "no government information" involved. Reuters was unable to independently authenticate the leaked emails, but noted that the personal Gmail address Handala claims to have accessed matches an account linked to Patel in previous data breaches documented by dark web intelligence firm District 4 Labs. Google, which operates Gmail, did not respond to a request for comment.

Handala presents itself as a pro-Palestinian vigilante hacking collective but is assessed by Western researchers to be one of several public personas operated by Iranian government cyberintelligence units. The group has claimed a series of recent operations, including a March 11 hack of Michigan-based medical device firm Stryker and a Thursday release of personal data belonging to dozens of Lockheed Martin employees stationed in the Middle East. Lockheed Martin said it was aware of the reports and had procedures in place to address cyber threats.

The attack on Patel fits a broader pattern of escalating Iranian cyber activity following coordinated U.S. and Israeli military strikes against Iran last month. Gil Messing, chief of staff at Israeli cybersecurity firm Check Point, said the hack-and-leak operation was part of Iran's strategy to embarrass senior U.S. officials and "make them feel vulnerable," adding that Iranian actors are "firing whatever they have." Targeting senior officials' personal email accounts is a known tactic among state-linked hackers, most notably employed when adversaries breached the personal Gmail account of Democratic operative John Podesta during the 2016 U.S. presidential campaign.
